quiz_questions.question_text: Systole refers to:

options.text: Heart relaxation and filling
Heart contraction and blood ejection
Heart arrest
Heart enlargement

quiz_questions.explanation: Systole is the phase of the cardiac cycle during which the ventricles contract, generating pressure to eject blood into the pulmonary artery and aorta.

quiz_questions.question_text: Diastole refers to:

options.text: Heart contraction
Heart relaxation and ventricular filling
A heart murmur
Cardiac arrest

quiz_questions.explanation: Diastole is the phase when the heart muscle relaxes, allowing the ventricles to fill with blood from the atria in preparation for the next contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: The SA node is called the pacemaker because it:

options.text: Is an artificial device implanted in the heart
Initiates each heartbeat by generating electrical impulses at 60-100 bpm
Is located in the ventricle
Responds to conscious voluntary control

quiz_questions.explanation: The sinoatrial (SA) node is a cluster of specialized cells in the right atrium that spontaneously depolarizes at the fastest intrinsic rate, setting the pace for the entire heart.

quiz_questions.question_text: The AV node's main role is to:

options.text: Initiate the heartbeat before the SA node
Briefly delay the impulse so atria finish emptying before ventricles contract
Speed up conduction to the atria
Produce red blood cells

quiz_questions.explanation: The atrioventricular (AV) node introduces a brief delay of about 0.1 seconds in impulse conduction, ensuring that the atria have time to fully contract and empty their blood into the ventricles before ventricular systole begins.

quiz_questions.question_text: On an ECG, the P wave represents:

options.text: Ventricular contraction
Atrial depolarization (atrial contraction)
Ventricular repolarization
Heart at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: The P wave is the first deflection on the ECG and represents the wave of depolarization spreading across both atria, triggering atrial contraction and blood flow into the ventricles.

quiz_questions.question_text: The QRS complex on an ECG represents:

options.text: Atrial depolarization
Ventricular depolarization (ventricular contraction)
Atrial repolarization
The resting heart between beats

quiz_questions.explanation: The QRS complex is the largest waveform on the ECG because the ventricles have considerably more muscle mass than the atria, producing a greater electrical signal during depolarization.

quiz_questions.question_text: The T wave on an ECG represents:

options.text: Atrial depolarization
Ventricular contraction
Ventricular repolarization (ventricles recovering)
Atrial repolarization

quiz_questions.explanation: The T wave represents ventricular repolarization, the electrical recovery phase during which the ventricular muscle cells reset their membrane potential in preparation for the next depolarization cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Normal resting heart rate for an adult is:

options.text: 200+ bpm

quiz_questions.explanation: A healthy adult resting heart rate ranges from 60 to 100 beats per minute, though well-conditioned athletes may have resting rates below 60 bpm due to increased cardiac efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: The sympathetic nervous system affects heart rate by:

options.text: Decreasing heart rate
Increasing heart rate and contractility
Having no effect on heart rate
Stopping the heart temporarily

quiz_questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system activates the fight-or-flight response by releasing norepinephrine, which increases both heart rate (chronotropy) and contractile force (inotropy) to boost cardiac output during stress or exercise.

quiz_questions.question_text: The parasympathetic nervous system affects heart rate via the vagus nerve by:

options.text: Increasing heart rate
Decreasing heart rate
Having no effect
Stopping the heart permanently

quiz_questions.explanation: The parasympathetic nervous system, acting through the vagus nerve and releasing acetylcholine, slows heart rate by reducing the firing rate of the SA node, promoting the rest-and-digest state.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is cardiac output (CO)?

options.text: The heart rate measured in beats per minute
The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle per minute, calculated as heart rate multiplied by stroke volume
The blood pressure measured in mmHg
The total blood volume in the body

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac output (CO = HR x SV) represents the total volume of blood pumped by a ventricle each minute, typically about 5 liters per minute at rest, and is a key indicator of overall cardiac performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is stroke volume?

options.text: The total blood pumped per minute
The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle per beat, approximately 70 mL
The heart rate multiplied by blood pressure
The total blood volume in the circulatory system

quiz_questions.explanation: Stroke volume is the difference between end-diastolic volume (blood in the ventricle at the end of filling) and end-systolic volume (blood remaining after contraction), averaging about 70 mL per beat at rest.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is bradycardia?

options.text: A heart rate above 100 bpm
A heart rate below 60 bpm
A normal heart rate of 60-100 bpm
An irregular heart rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: Bradycardia is defined as a heart rate below 60 bpm; it can be normal in well-trained athletes but may be pathological if it causes symptoms like dizziness, fatigue, or syncope due to reduced cardiac output.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is tachycardia?

options.text: A heart rate below 60 bpm
A heart rate above 100 bpm
A normal resting heart rate
A heart with no detectable rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: Tachycardia is a heart rate exceeding 100 bpm at rest; while it can be a normal response to exercise, fever, or stress, sustained tachycardia at rest may indicate an underlying cardiac or systemic problem.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is atrial fibrillation (AFib)?

options.text: A normal heart rhythm originating from the SA node
Chaotic, irregular atrial electrical activity producing an irregularly irregular ventricular response
A complete cessation of heart activity
A valve disorder causing regurgitation

quiz_questions.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, characterized by rapid, disorganized atrial electrical activity that replaces normal P waves, leading to an irregular ventricular rate and increased risk of blood clots and stroke.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is ventricular fibrillation (VFib)?

options.text: A mild arrhythmia that resolves on its own
A life-threatening arrhythmia with no effective cardiac pumping, causing cardiac arrest
A normal finding during exercise
A slow but regular heart rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: Ventricular fibrillation is a lethal arrhythmia in which the ventricles quiver chaotically instead of contracting effectively, producing no cardiac output; it is a medical emergency requiring immediate defibrillation to restore a viable rhythm.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the Purkinje fiber system do?

options.text: Generates the initial heartbeat impulse
Rapidly conducts electrical impulses throughout the ventricular walls for synchronized contraction
Slows conduction between the atria and ventricles
Produces hormones that regulate heart rate

quiz_questions.explanation: The Purkinje fibers are a network of specialized conduction cells that rapidly distribute the electrical impulse from the bundle branches throughout the ventricular myocardium, ensuring both ventricles contract in a coordinated, efficient manner.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'intercalated disc' mean in cardiac muscle?

options.text: A type of heart valve between chambers
Specialized cell junctions between cardiac muscle cells that contain gap junctions for electrical syncytium
The fibrous rings around the heart valves
A layer of connective tissue in the pericardium

quiz_questions.explanation: Intercalated discs are unique junctions between cardiac muscle cells containing desmosomes (for mechanical attachment) and gap junctions (for rapid electrical signal transmission), allowing the heart to function as a coordinated electrical syncytium.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Bundle of His?

options.text: A group of pacemaker cells in the right atrium
A conduction pathway that transmits impulses from the AV node through the interventricular septum to the bundle branches
A nerve that connects the brain to the heart
A valve that prevents backflow between the ventricles

quiz_questions.explanation: The Bundle of His is the only normal electrical pathway connecting the atria to the ventricles, conducting impulses from the AV node through the interventricular septum where it divides into left and right bundle branches.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'autorhythmicity' mean in cardiac physiology?

options.text: The heart's dependence on the brain for each heartbeat
The ability of cardiac cells to generate their own electrical impulses without external stimulation
The regulation of heart rate by hormones only
The ability of the heart to change size

quiz_questions.explanation: Autorhythmicity is the intrinsic ability of certain cardiac cells (especially SA node cells) to spontaneously depolarize and generate action potentials without nervous system input, which is why the heart continues to beat even when removed from the body.

quiz_questions.question_text: A well-trained marathon runner has a resting heart rate of 48 bpm but reports no symptoms of dizziness or fatigue. Should this be treated as pathological bradycardia?

options.text: Yes, any heart rate below 60 bpm requires immediate treatment
No, athletes often have lower resting rates because their hearts pump more blood per beat (higher stroke volume)
Yes, the athlete needs an artificial pacemaker
No, but only because the runner is young

quiz_questions.explanation: Athletic training increases stroke volume through cardiac remodeling, so the heart needs fewer beats per minute to maintain adequate cardiac output; asymptomatic sinus bradycardia in athletes is a normal physiological adaptation, not a disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient in the emergency department collapses and the cardiac monitor shows a chaotic, irregular waveform with no identifiable P waves, QRS complexes, or T waves. What is the rhythm and what is the immediate treatment?

options.text: Atrial fibrillation; administer blood thinners immediately
Ventricular fibrillation; perform immediate defibrillation
Sinus bradycardia; administer atropine
Normal sinus rhythm; no treatment needed

quiz_questions.explanation: Ventricular fibrillation produces a chaotic ECG with no organized waveforms and no cardiac output; without immediate defibrillation to reset the heart's electrical activity, the patient will die within minutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician calculates that a patient has a heart rate of 80 bpm and a stroke volume of 60 mL. What is this patient's cardiac output, and is it within normal range?

options.text: 4,800 mL/min (4.8 L/min), which is within the normal range of about 4-8 L/min
140 mL/min, which is dangerously low
8,000 mL/min, which is dangerously high
480 mL/min, which indicates heart failure

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac output = HR x SV = 80 bpm x 60 mL = 4,800 mL/min (4.8 L/min), which falls within the normal resting range of approximately 4-8 L/min and indicates adequate cardiac function.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with a history of atrial fibrillation is prescribed warfarin (a blood thinner). Why does AFib increase the risk of blood clots forming?

options.text: AFib causes the ventricles to pump too forcefully, damaging vessel walls
The atria quiver instead of contracting effectively, allowing blood to stagnate and clot, especially in the left atrial appendage
AFib produces excessive platelets that aggregate spontaneously
AFib raises blood pressure to levels that damage the endothelium

quiz_questions.explanation: In atrial fibrillation, the atria quiver chaotically instead of contracting, causing blood to pool and stagnate, particularly in the left atrial appendage, creating conditions favorable for thrombus formation that can embolize to the brain causing a stroke.

quiz_questions.question_text: An elderly patient is brought to the ER feeling dizzy and lightheaded. The ECG shows a heart rate of 35 bpm with a normal P-QRS-T pattern. The physician decides to insert a temporary pacemaker. What is the likely diagnosis?

options.text: Atrial fibrillation
Symptomatic sinus bradycardia likely due to SA node dysfunction
Ventricular tachycardia
Normal athletic bradycardia

quiz_questions.explanation: A heart rate of 35 bpm with symptoms of dizziness indicates symptomatic sinus bradycardia, likely from SA node dysfunction (sick sinus syndrome); because the patient is symptomatic with inadequate cardiac output, a pacemaker is needed to maintain an adequate rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nursing student is monitoring a patient and notices that the ECG shows progressively lengthening PR intervals until a QRS complex is dropped. What conduction abnormality is this?

options.text: First-degree AV block
Second-degree AV block, Type I (Wenckebach)
Third-degree (complete) heart block
Atrial fibrillation

quiz_questions.explanation: Second-degree AV block Type I (Wenckebach) is characterized by progressive prolongation of the PR interval with each successive beat until one impulse is completely blocked and a QRS is 'dropped,' after which the cycle resets.

quiz_questions.question_text: A firefighter responding to a call experiences a sudden surge in heart rate from 72 to 140 bpm. Which division of the autonomic nervous system is responsible for this rapid change?

options.text: The parasympathetic nervous system via increased vagal tone
The sympathetic nervous system releasing norepinephrine to increase rate and contractility
The somatic nervous system via voluntary muscle control
The enteric nervous system regulating digestive blood flow

quiz_questions.explanation: During the stress response, the sympathetic nervous system releases norepinephrine and the adrenal medulla releases epinephrine, both of which act on the SA node and myocardium to increase heart rate and contractile force, preparing the body for physical exertion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is learning about the conduction system and asks why the entire ventricle does not contract from the top down. What feature of the conduction system ensures the ventricles contract from the apex upward?

options.text: The SA node sends signals directly to the ventricular apex
The Purkinje fibers distribute the impulse first to the apex, causing contraction to begin at the bottom and squeeze blood upward toward the outflow tracts
Gravity pulls the electrical signal downward
The AV node delays all impulses equally across the ventricle

quiz_questions.explanation: The bundle branches travel down the interventricular septum and the Purkinje fibers spread from the apex upward, so depolarization begins at the ventricular apex and progresses upward, efficiently squeezing blood toward the semilunar valves and outflow tracts.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient in the intensive care unit is septic with a heart rate of 130 bpm. Despite the fast rate, cardiac output is low and blood pressure is falling. A cardiologist explains that the heart is not filling adequately. Why does extreme tachycardia reduce cardiac output?

options.text: The heart valves cannot open fast enough during tachycardia
Very fast rates shorten diastole so much that the ventricles have inadequate time to fill, reducing stroke volume
Tachycardia causes the AV node to block all impulses
Fast heart rates destroy red blood cells, reducing oxygen delivery

quiz_questions.explanation: At very high heart rates, the diastolic filling period becomes critically shortened because more time is spent in systole; with insufficient filling time, end-diastolic volume and stroke volume drop, which can paradoxically reduce cardiac output despite the rapid rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A paramedic arrives at a scene where a person has collapsed. The AED (automated external defibrillator) analyzes the rhythm and advises a shock. The victim is successfully resuscitated. Why was electrical defibrillation effective in this scenario?

options.text: The shock directly strengthened the weakened heart muscle
The electrical shock simultaneously depolarized all cardiac cells, momentarily stopping the chaotic rhythm so the SA node could resume normal pacemaker function
The shock restarted the heart after it had completely stopped
The shock destroyed the abnormal cells causing the arrhythmia

quiz_questions.explanation: Defibrillation works by delivering a massive electrical current that depolarizes all myocardial cells simultaneously, resetting the chaotic electrical activity and giving the SA node the opportunity to resume its role as the dominant pacemaker with an organized rhythm.
